pred_reason: The SWC-105 label is appropriate because the contract contains unprotected functions that allow arbitrary ownership transfer initiation. This violates access control requirements by permitting unauthorized state changes through direct function calls. Evidence: Duplicate transferOwnership functions: one protected with onlyOwner, one unprotected; Any address can initiate ownership transfer, enabling complete contract takeover; Impact includes draining accidentally sent ERC20 tokens and gaining all owner privileges

findings.Issue: Critical Access Control Vulnerability in Ownership Transfer
findings.Description: The contract has duplicate transferOwnership functions - one protected with onlyOwner modifier and one unprotected. This allows any address to initiate ownership transfer, potentially leading to complete contract takeover. This issue appears in the smart contract code only.
findings.Impact: Complete contract takeover, allowing attacker to drain accidentally sent ERC20 tokens and gain all owner privileges

findings.Description: The acceptOwnership function fails to reset newOwner to address(0) after successful ownership transfer, allowing previously proposed owners to hijack ownership later. This issue appears in the smart contract code only.
findings.Impact: Potential ownership hijacking even after legitimate transfers, leading to complete contract control by malicious actors

findings.Description: Standard ERC-20 allowance race condition vulnerability where malicious spenders can front-run approval changes to use both old and new allowances. This is a known ERC-20 standard issue, not a code bug. Appears in the smart contract code as standard implementation. This issue appears in the auditing result.
findings.Impact: Users may unintentionally allow spenders to use more tokens than intended, potentially leading to financial loss

findings.Description: The contract is susceptible to front-running attacks, particularly in the approve function where spenders can front-run allowance changes. This appears in the smart contract code as standard ERC-20 implementation.
findings.Impact: Malicious spenders can withdraw more tokens than intended by exploiting transaction ordering

findings.Description: The totalSupply() function assumes all burned tokens are sent to address(0), but if tokens are burned to other addresses, the supply calculation becomes inaccurate. This issue appears in the smart contract code only.
findings.Impact: Inaccurate token supply reporting, which could mislead external systems relying on accurate supply data

llm_result.reason: Unprotected ownership transfer functions allow arbitrary address to initiate ownership change, leading to potential unauthorized withdrawals if owner privileges are exploited.
